items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: What is the difference between gross profit and net profit?
items.correct: Gross profit = revenue minus cost of goods sold (direct costs only); net profit = revenue minus ALL costs including overheads, interest and tax. Net profit is the bottom line.
items.misconception: Gross profit and net profit are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Gross profit is profit before VAT and net profit is profit after VAT.
items.why: The gap between gross and net reveals overhead burden. A high gross but low net margin means heavy fixed costs or financing. Students conflate the two and misread profitability.

items.q: Why might a business adopt corporate social responsibility (CSR)?
items.correct: Reasons include genuine ethics, but also commercial benefits — improved reputation, customer loyalty, employee retention, and reduced regulatory or reputational risk.
items.misconception: CSR is purely about being ethical with no business benefit.
items.random_wrong: CSR is the legal requirement to pay taxes.
items.why: CSR is both ethical and strategic. Students often treat it as either pure morality or pure cynicism — in business studies the balanced answer recognises both motivations.
The answer examiners want is: Just-in-time. “Job inventory tracking” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Continuous improvement. “Cost cutting” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If actual output is 800 and maximum is 1000, what is capacity utilisation (%)?
items.why: Exact answer: 80 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
